speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two-- it's December 11, 1995. We're interviewing Manny Flaster in San Diego, California. We were talking about when you did your apprenticeship for tailoring. What year did you start doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? There were-- that was in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- the tailoring I was doing in Krynica-Zdroj. That is a-- a place where the-- for summer, you know, and winter. That was two seasons in that place-- was very, very beautiful-- was in the mountains. And the people came over there, you know, just for relaxation-- and over there where I did the tailoring. It was 28 kilometers from my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you live with when you were doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother had a-- a business over there what-- I lived with my brother, with my oldest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go home often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, only on the holidays. Matter of fact, the first year, I remember when I was-- had to go home, that was in January, where my boss didn't had so much work. He didn't wanted to keep me, because he give me lunches already. So he send me home. And I didn't have money to-- for the train. I didn't have money for the horse and buggy to there. So I walked 28 kilometers-- snow there, you know, almost covered me, the-- the-- because it was go through mountains, you know. And the snow was so deep, one leg in-- [LAUGHS] --one leg out. I start early morning to work. I came home late at night. The whole day I walked in order to come home. [LAUGHS] All things like this I will not forget this. It was such a rough life, but now it is interesting to remember what it was, and what, thank God, it is now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things began happening around the town before the actual war started that made you feel something was wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the-- when the war started, I-- me and my brothers ran away from the house. We left my father, my mother, and mine two sisters at home. We thought we're-- we will run deeper to Poland, what the-- the Germans will be stopped, because, you know, the radios always, you know, said how strong Poland is, and Poland will stop them, you know, when they called, and then things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we ran deeper to Poland, but it was not so. When the Germans came in, they just went through Poland without a fight, practically. And when we came, already they owe us about 250 to 300 kilometers away. We went there-- day in and day night-- day and night.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came. There were-- as far as [NON-ENGLISH]. But this is already quite a bit away. The Germans say they got us around, but we couldn't go no place. The Germans was already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to go back to home. And the minute when we can back home, we've seen already what the hell started. Because like the Germans start to taking the Jewish people to work, taking the Orthodox Jewish people, cutting their beards, cutting their payos. There's a beating them, killing them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so soon I came home with my brothers. And I had my older brother, what is now in Paris, France. He had-- where he was working, he had the boys and girls afraid, what they-- they said they cannot stay there and work for the Germans. So they said they-- they will go across the other 1/2 of Poland. Because at that time, the Russian took already 1/2 Poland. And the Germans had-- had the other 1/2. So they went to the border, and they were-- went over the border, what was water over there. They swim that through, and-- to be on the Russian sector.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brother. My brother what is Paris, France. I stayed at home. I couldn't go no place anymore. I had to wear a-- imagine from the walking-- I had my feet swollen until it was infected, but I couldn't hardly walk. But my brother right away left. And then my other brother left. So they went to the Russian sector. Me, they right away, the Germans, took me to work in the city-- even I was sick, but they didn't care. They told me that I had to chop wood for the German soldiers over there for the kitchen to cook, or to sweep all the streets and things like this-- all kind of work in the city what I had to work every day for the Germans. That was right in the beginning. Later, about a month later, or maybe more than this, the first snow fall down was a very heavy snow-- was a couple feet of snow. So the train couldn't go through. So they took us, all Jewish people from the city, to clean all the-- the train-- the-- the-- dig where the train goes. We-- we start, and I remember that's Thursday to-- to sweep it and to clean the-- over there. When we finished, let's say, a couple kilometers, but the snow was so fast that we had to go back and start from the beginning. So the-- the-- the job was Thursday-- Thursday day, Thursday night, Friday a whole day. Friday afternoon, when the-- the snow stopped, this-- they let us home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they let us home, I went right away to bed to the-- to sleep. Sure enough, in the evening, they knocked on the door, and they took me out again to clean the snow. Because it start snowing again. And we had to work for several days till the snow stopped. We had to work on the-- the thing to clean the snow off where the trains could go through. Because they were very busy, you know, with the soldiers, transporting different ways. And then later, you know, the-- the different works we had to-- to do in the city-- finally, December the 12th, that-- a few-- a few days before, they give out-- how to say this in English-- you know, the-- what-- we had to be on a place-- and mine age-- that is from 18 years to 25 years. We had to be on a place what they will take us to work in a camp. And they promised us they will give-- pay us, they will treat us good. And we will have nothing what to worry about. We had no choice. But they threatened us. If we won't come over there, they'll find somebody that age group that will kill us. So we went over there. They put us on a truck. That was December the 12th what they took us away from home to a-- the first labor camp. And that was [NON-ENGLISH], not far from [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the last time you saw your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was not the last time. Because I'd worked in a camp what on every Sunday, I'd run away from camp. But I came home, because I had to take a bath and things like this. But that's in-- although you see, that was-- the first camp, we were working over there. And we thought if we finished the work, they would let us go, they will send us back home. But it wasn't so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So soon if we-- didn't even finish the work, because it got frozen so hard-- the ground. We had to build roads, but the ground got frozen so hard we had to-- it was impossible for us to work. They sent us to another camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that other camp was Lipie. We came over there, and there was no water even to wash up. They brought in water to the kitchen by horse and buggy, by big bales. So there were some boys what they were-- were dirty. They stole water in the kitchen, and they were selling that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was buying water for my portion bread, what we had got a little bit of bread. I cut it in 1/2 and gived it the rest. I bought water to wash up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, that wasn't enough for me. I-- I was very clean. I was, you know, brought up, you know, to cleanliness is next to godliness. So I couldn't stand it. So I found a way through the mountains where I could save about at least 45% of the [NON-ENGLISH] in order to go home. Saturday, we finished the work in the afternoon. We didn't work Sunday-- just stayed in the barracks. So while we finished the work, I didn't went back to the barracks, I went through the mountains home, what I could take a bath and change mine clothes-- that I feel clean. When I come back when they made the appell-- the appell just means they were reading the names Sunday morning. And I was missing. And there were a couple more guys were missing. When we came back Monday morning, we got 25 lashes. But I was a very stubborn young man. I always said they will not break my morale, mine-- my will. They will-- they will not do to me. That's what they want me to do. Because that camp was already very dirty. It was bugs, you know, biting and thing like this. But we had to keep ourselves clean. And I couldn't stand that to be in that camp and not to go home at least once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So came Saturday, I did the same thing. I went home. I came home, I took my bath, changed clothes, and-- and I know I come back, I will get 25 lashes. I didn't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, I got tired. I was-- so I didn't went every week. I went every second week. But I still went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So long we were in that camp-- this was 1940-- I did not listen to them. I didn't care what they say. I went home every second week or every week-- depends, you know, I felt-- in order to take the bath on there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And mine mother, she already waited for me. You know, she had prepared a hot bath, she had prepared everything. When I came home, you know, I got it everything ready. Because I couldn't take off the clothes in the house, because it was soaked with the lice and all kind of bugs from the camp. What I had to take off the clothes outside and right away to put the clothes in the hot water, you know, to wash it. And-- and for me to take a bath and to change clothes was, you know, it was a very, very humiliating-- it was very, you know, disgusting, what-- that kind of conditions what they put us through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the barracks look like? Can you describe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were the big barracks with-- with the bunk beds, you know, three, the-- three stories bunk beds. On the-- only straw was on the beds-- no-- nothing, you know, fancy. It was long. But in a barrack you could sleep up to 250 to 300 people in one barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they feed you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] They gave us in the morning black coffee with one piece of bread-- what was a few ounces-- maybe it were about three ounces of bread-- with a piece of margarine-- not all the time the margarine. The bread with the coffee was the every day. The margarine, sometimes we got it. And in the evening, just a little bit soup-- very, very thin soup, what-- that hot water, like they say-- was better than nothing. That's all food what we got. And with that food, we had to work very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you building there, or doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that particular camp, again, we were making roads, pushing mountain away. We were digging stones from the mountains. We had this-- on-- in that particular camp, we laid down in the mountains-- it was a mountain about 10 stories high. And we had a ledge like this to stand up and to digging out the stones from the mountains, throw them down, down the-- down such a way-- people with the-- breaking them up, put them in a machine where the machine ground it. That was to make the roads-- the stones. On that mountain, many people died, because when somebody fall down from that ledge, was nothing more to pick up. They-- they didn't even bother to go to pick him up, because he was gone. It was so high and so treacherous that it was not worth to pick up. On that mountain, I-- one time, where I'd almost fall down also, because I hit myself right here with a pick-axe, you know, what we digged out the stones-- but went in here, and came out there, you know, and I'd-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --but when that happened-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --when that happened, I'd felt very, very bad pain. And I just grabbed myself at the soles not to fall down. And I hardly could go ahead, you know? The-- that-- where I had somebody had to help you to go down and to go to a-- back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there, they-- we didn't had any doctors. So he give me the three days to recuperate-- not to go back. Because I couldn't walk on the leg. It-- it was very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he give me three days. In the three days, I took a permission to go home. So I came home, and that didn't healed. Matter of fact, it got worse, because I didn't have any vitamins in me. I didn't have anything, you know, to-- any medication what that-- to make it heal. So that got worse. And as that got worse, I didn't want to go-- I-- go back to the camp. I ran away from the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I-- as I didn't went back, the police start looking for me. And when I was at the farmer's, you know, I know farmers from before the war. Well, I went to them, and I ask them to keep me over there and to give me to eat, only to eat, you know, and I would do all kind of work for them. They needed tailoring, you know, like fixing up for children and for themselves and others. And so long as they had the work for me, they kept me. But later, they want to give me back to the police. And I see that. Every time, I was watching them. When I finished the work, I watched them. And so sure enough, they send their neighbor, you know, to bring the police. I went out through a window for-- and went to a-- to a-- the high tree, you know. And I-- and I hid over there till, you know, that's went by. Police came. They looked for me everywhere. They couldn't find me. So I came down, and I went to another farm. I did this for seven weeks. After the seven weeks, I thought it was already quiet. I didn't had any more places to go. So I came home on a Saturday, but I want to go to the synagogue for the Saturday prayers. As I was walking to the synagogue, a Polish policeman recognized me and then put me to jail. When he put me to jail-- so at that time, it was a law on the Germans what if they find somebody outside the city, outside the-- the camp, they'll right away be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had mine older brother still at home. So he went over there to the-- to the city, and he bribed the Gestapo man what he was the-- the one to-- to tell me I live or die. He bribed him to somebody what knowed him, you know. It was a whole thing. For money, you could do anything, even with the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came over there, they caught three more boys the same day what also they ran away from the camp. And all four of us, we came. So I was the longest away, so I got 85 lashes. But they didn't kill me, just they gave me 85 lashes. And they send me back to the camp. I had to walk 13 or 15 kilometers, whatever it was, over there. When I came back to the camp, I got 25 more. So I got, in one day, I had 110 lashes. And they put me to the hardest work over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they hit you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had made special sticks, you know. It was out of rubber. And in-- in the middle was a wire-- wires, you know, what-- that was flexible. If not, they were made out of leather and also wires in the middle. Every time they hit, you know, they cut the skin, you know, they say. It was painful. It was very, very painful. To go back in that-- in that-- in that particular camp, when they moved a little bit further-- because we finished the work on one site-- so a few kilometers further south-- they moved away the camp. Over there, not far, about a few hundred feet was a lake. In the wintertime, the lake was frozen. You see, that was already time where I did not wanna go any more home when I had a lake. I could wash in the-- in the lake. But in the wintertime, when it was frozen, I made a hole in the ice. And I went in in the ice-- in-- in the ice cold water, and I took a bath in the ice cold water there. And I went back to the camp for the things. I-- I did things, you know-- I'm telling you what-- if I wake up at night, and I remember the things what I did, I don't believe myself what I was able to do the things what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that camp, we were about 11 months or something like this, maybe even more considering-- quite a long-- 9, 10, 11 months-- something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear your own clothes in these camps, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that camp, we had our own clothes, yes. And this is another story. It came wintertime with already the first snowfall down-- with ice. The lake was frozen. Of course, it was, you know, snow already. And-- and to go to work, the shoe of mine got torn. I didn't have any shoes. So I told them to give me a pair of shoes. They said oh, you maybe-- you-- you still have a home. You can maybe take home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, that a home, I didn't have. I really won't take my last pair of shoes from my mother or my father. Well, what are you talking about? So they say, well, we give you a pair of shoes. What they-- they wanted to do is to take a pair of shoes from the sick people, what they were sick, and give it to me to wear. And I felt-- I was very righteous all the time. And I felt I wear their shoes, I will tear them down, you know. And then when you go and then there's-- you not working and-- a few days, the shoes were lasting that I would turn down that the young man when he get well, he won't have any shoes. So why should I wear his shoes? I do the work for them. Let them give me a pair of shoes. So I'd always had, you know, something to say-- what the shoes are no good. The shoes either too small, too big, or whatever. I didn't took him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't went to work. I stayed in the barrack. Because barefoot to go work, you know, when in the snow, and it was bad weather and things like this, I didn't went to work. And I finally started-- other people were in the same situation as I was. So I told them to do this, what I am doing. Finally, we got to-- they saw, worked about 40 people stayed in the barrack-- didn't went to work. And they needed us, you know, to-- to do the work. So finally, they got disgusted. They called out the Gestapo man. And the Gestapo man, he had another man, a very big, strong man, with a German shepherd. And that man, he was the one what was doing the beating. So when the Gestapo man came out, start calling in the boys-- known. The-- somewhere, some guy didn't work two days-- give them 5 lashes, 10 days, you know. So it depends how many days he didn't work-- so many lashes he-- they got.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come in the last. They called me in the last. So he ask, is that's the one what is started all trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say yes, this is the one. He got up. And he was not a small guy. He was a-- a big man. And he slapped me over the face with all his might. He slapped me. I stayed straight. I looked him straight in the eyes. I didn't even blink with the eye.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, jeez, he is a tough fellow. So he says to him, give him 125. So I got 125.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I note what the dog was so trained, he was standing and-- just making, you know, barking at me. Even if I would move, and I would scream, the dog bite you. He was so trained, what the dog will bite.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought to myself, I don't want to be bitten by the dog. So I just bit my lips together. I took the 125 lashes, and I walked out straight, just to make him really mad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me in German, [GERMAN]. That means, if he will have to come back once more here to the camp, he will shoot me himself. I didn't say nothing. I just walked. Well, I came back to the barracks. So I had my friends that put cold water on me, you know, with ice, and, you know, the-- the-- towels, you know, with the-- I won't get any fever. Because my whole back was ripped apart practically. I then, next day, at camp, I still didn't go to work. I said, you can call him back and let him kill me. I will not go to work till you will bring shoes for all of us to work. The other boys, they were afraid. So some of them went back. But about 10, 12 of them stayed with me. They said whatever happened to me will happen to them. And they stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about four or five days later, a truck brought shoes for everybody to wear. The shoes were-- the top was leather, the bottom was wood. But at least it was something to wear. So I won the fight, but not the war. But-- but I didn't give in. As I said before to you, I was a very stubborn guy. I know my rights. Even I was the youngest in the camp, but the people looked up to me, the things what I did. Yeah. How old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was 31 years old. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not what I say-- 31-- 21 years old. Because I was-- this was the second year-- the 21 years old I was. I was very, very stubborn-- very stubborn. I-- in that camp, you know, I made songs, you know, because in the evening, you know, the-- you were always thinking about the-- the hunger, how hungry you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was with another boy, and with him put songs together. We put the lyrics in and then the melody. And we made songs what-- about the life of the camp. Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I remember it directly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I know you won't understand. It's in-- because they're Jewish. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can translate it, maybe, for us after you sing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is a song where I am asking the other boy what the [NON-ENGLISH] is is his name-- by asking him how he sleeps. And he says no, he doesn't sleep, because the lice and the fleas bites him so much what you know, it's impossible to sleep. It goes like this. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: That says, you know, when I ask him then, and he tells me this. And then he ask, oh my friends, how can I stand this old problems? And later is--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] And that says, you know, he-- he's telling me what he got only a little piece of bread, you know, and he had to work so hard. And how can he get by, you know, when they ask how he can stand it. You know, it's a whole song, you know, it's-- it's much further-- [LAUGHS] --it goes, you know. Did that-- truly happy making this, and-- and these other songs what we were-- we were doing all kind of things in order to forget from the hunger, to forget the problems. Because the worst thing is, in a time like this, if you start thinking how bad you have it. You start feeling sorry for yourself-- you'll go off your-- your rocket.
